lead: A moving day in Sudbury goes far more smoothly with a clear plan, particularly in a Stour Valley town where the older central streets and former weavers' cottages can be tight for a removal lorry. After years of carrying homes into Sudbury from our base in Westerfield, we know exactly where the day tends to slow down and how to keep it flowing. This checklist takes you through the run-up, the morning itself and your first hours in your new Sudbury home, with the practical detail that keeps the whole day calm.

sections.h: Two Weeks Before: Plan and Book
sections.body:
The fortnight before your move is when the foundations are laid, and in Sudbury the first thing to think about is access. The town has a good number of older central streets and historic weavers' cottages with narrow approaches and limited parking, so if your new home is among them, work out early where a removal lorry can realistically stop. Tell us about it so we can plan, perhaps by bringing a smaller vehicle to shuttle from a larger one, or by timing the unload to avoid the busiest part of the day. If your new place is a more modern home towards the edge of town, access is usually far simpler.

This is also the time to confirm your moving date with us and to start running down the freezer and store cupboards. Notify the important people of your change of address, arrange mail redirection, and book any time off you will need. If your chain is looking uncertain, talk to us now about storage rather than leaving it to chance; we would much rather plan a calm short-term solution in advance than scramble if completion slips, which in any town it sometimes does.

Start gathering packing materials too, or ask us to supply them. Sturdy boxes, plenty of tape, bubble wrap for fragile items and marker pens for clear labelling all make a real difference, and running out partway through packing is a guaranteed frustration. Aim to have everything you need in the house before you begin in earnest, so the packing flows steadily rather than stalling every time you reach for materials that have run out.

sections.h: One Week Before: Pack Room by Room
sections.body:
With a week to go, packing should be well underway. Work methodically, room by room, and begin with the spaces you use least: the spare room, the loft, the garage and the shed. The loft is the one that catches everyone out, holding far more than people remember, and discovering heavy boxes up a ladder on moving morning throws the timetable out, which matters all the more if you have a tight unloading slot booked in central Sudbury. Clear those out-of-the-way spaces this week without fail.

Label every box clearly on the side as well as the top, noting the room it belongs to in your new Sudbury home and a quick summary of the contents. We carry boxes straight to the right room as we unload, so good labels keep everything from piling up in the hall. A colour-coding system works a treat: one colour per room, matching stickers on the boxes and a matching sheet on each door at the new house, so even a crew seeing the property for the first time can unload at speed, which is exactly what you want when time on a narrow street is at a premium.

Keep anything precious, important documents, jewellery and small valuables, aside to travel in your own car. Mark fragile boxes on more than one side, and keep a simple numbered inventory if any part of your move involves storage or a staggered handover. By the end of this week, the only things still to pack should be the kettle, the breakfast things and the beds you are sleeping in the final night.

The day before is for tying off the loose ends. Defrost and empty the fridge and freezer, finish the last of the packing, and strip all but tonight's beds, so that when we arrive everything is sealed and ready except the kettle and the breakfast things. The single biggest cause of a slow, costly moving day is unfinished packing when the crew turns up, and in Sudbury that delay can eat into a carefully arranged parking window on a tight central street, so being genuinely ready really does pay off here.

Pack your essentials box, the most important box of all, and set it aside for your own car rather than the lorry. Into it goes the kettle, mugs, tea, milk and biscuits, phone chargers, basic toiletries, a change of clothes, any medication, important documents and a few toys for the children. This is the box that keeps everyone going and lets you make a brew the moment you arrive without opening the whole van. Take and note your meter readings at the old property, and charge your phone overnight.

Think through the morning route as well. Sudbury's centre and market hill can get busy, and the older streets can be slow going for a large vehicle, so if your route passes through the heart of town a little timing thought tonight saves frustration tomorrow. Clear a path from the front door to the kerb at both ends, and if you have young children or pets, arrange care for the day or set up one already-cleared room as their safe base while everything else moves around them.

sections.h: Moving Morning: Working With the Crew
sections.body:
On the morning itself, the most valuable thing you can do is be ready and be present, especially in a town where unloading time can be limited. Open the curtains, switch on the lights so we can see, and make sure nothing is still hidden in the loft, shed or behind a door. When we arrive we like a quick walk-round so you can point out the fragile boxes, the antiques and anything needing extra care, which matters particularly if you are moving older or heavier furniture into a Sudbury cottage with narrow stairs and tight doorways.

Leave out a kettle, mugs and a few biscuits if you can; it keeps the team going and it is the small courtesy our crews always appreciate. Keep children and pets in their safe room with a clear note on the door, because the front door will stand open for much of the morning and cats especially will bolt given the chance. Tell us where they are so we all keep that door shut. Where parking is restricted, let us get straight to loading so we make the most of the available window.

Before we leave the old property, do a proper walk-through together. Check every cupboard, the loft, the shed, the garden and behind every door, because things get left in the rush. Confirm the inventory, sort the handover of the old keys and make sure you have the keys for your new Sudbury home. Then meet us at the other end, where we will unload to the right rooms and rebuild the beds so you have somewhere comfortable to sleep on your first night in the town.

sections.h: Arriving in Sudbury: The First Hours
sections.body:
Arriving at the new home is where all the careful labelling pays off. As we unload, we carry each box to the room marked on its side, so even with a tight unloading window your home quickly takes shape rather than becoming one enormous pile in the hall. Point us to where the big furniture goes before we bring it in; placing a wardrobe or sofa correctly first time is far easier than shuffling it once it is full, and in an older cottage with awkward corners that little bit of planning really counts. Colour-coded doors make the whole unload faster and calmer.

Get the kitchen working enough for a brew and a simple meal, and ask us to make up the beds before we leave. After a long day, the difference between a made-up bed and a mattress buried under boxes is the difference between a good first night in Sudbury and a miserable one. Find your essentials box, put the kettle on, and resist the temptation to unpack everything tonight. The boxes will still be there in the morning, and a proper rest is the best possible start in your new home.

Over the following days, unpack at a sensible pace, room by room, and take time to enjoy where you now live. A first walk out across the water meadows by the River Stour, a look around the Gainsborough gallery and a wander up the market hill on a trading day are how a new house starts to feel like home. Register with a local GP and dentist, sort your utility and council accounts, and let yourself settle. We will have done our part by then, and every load we carry is fully covered, so you can put your energy into enjoying life in Sudbury.

faqs: When should I start packing for my Sudbury move?

Begin around two weeks out with the rooms you use least, such as the spare room, loft and garage, and work methodically room by room from there. Aim to have everything except the kettle and the beds packed by the day before, so our crew can load straight away on the morning.
What access issues should I check for in Sudbury?

Many of Sudbury's older central streets and former weavers' cottages have narrow access and limited parking. Work out where a lorry can stop and tell us in advance so we can plan, possibly using a smaller shuttle vehicle. Newer homes towards the edge of town are usually far simpler.
What should go in my essentials box for a Sudbury move?

Pack the kettle, mugs, tea, milk and biscuits, phone chargers, basic toiletries, a change of clothes, any medication, important documents and a few toys for children. Keep it in your own car so it is to hand the moment you reach your new Sudbury home.
Can you move heavy furniture into an older Sudbury cottage?

Yes. We regularly move heavy and older furniture into period cottages with narrow stairs and tight doorways. Point these pieces out on the morning walk-round so we can pad them and plan the carry, and the move goes smoothly even in the trickier historic Sudbury homes.
